items.mistake: Assuming gradient zero always means you reached a minimum
items.fix: Check the Hessian (or use a second-order test): indefinite curvature indicates a saddle; only positive definite implies a local minimum in smooth unconstrained settings.
items.why: Non-convex losses have many critical points that are not minima; early stopping on small gradient norm can hide saddles.
items.example: For f(x,y)=x^2-y^2, gradient is [0,0] at the origin but the Hessian eigenvalues are +2 and -2—a saddle, not a minimum.

items.mistake: Calling every wavy function non-convex without checking the definition
items.fix: Apply the chord test or Hessian positive semidefiniteness; some functions look irregular yet remain convex (e.g., x^2), while others like x^4 - x^2 are genuinely non-convex.
items.why: Convexity is a global shape property, not merely having more than one critical point.
items.example: f(x)=x^4 is convex (Hessian 12x^2 >= 0) even though its graph is flat near zero; g(x)=x^4 - x^2 is non-convex (Hessian negative at x=0).

items.mistake: Treating a local minimum found in training as the global optimum
items.fix: For non-convex models, compare multiple seeds, track validation metrics, and treat the result as one feasible solution unless the problem is known convex.
items.why: Different initializations routinely reach different training losses on the same architecture.
items.example: Train three random seeds for an MLP; if losses are 0.42, 0.38, and 0.51, you likely hit different basins—not comparable global optima.
